title: Petticoat tails
subtitle: Scottish shortbread shaped as a wheel and scored into wedges
excerpt: Scottish shortbread pressed into a round and scored into wedges before baking. No cutter needed. The milk binds the dough cleanly and the edges stay crisp. Makes eight wedges.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): petticoat tails documented as a traditional Scottish shortbread shaped in the old French style, cut into segments like a farthingale. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the same wheel-shaped shortbread appears under the Scots biscuit entries. The use of a small amount of milk to bind the dough is a consistent feature of the older Scottish form, distinguishing it from the purely dry-pressed shortbread made in the twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and fully cooled. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe and bake two rounds side by side on one large tray.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be wrapped and refrigerated for up to 48 hours.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 160°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: Making shallow cuts into dough or batter before baking to define shapes, control where the surface cracks, or allow steam to escape. In shortbread, scoring the wheel into wedges while the dough is raw means the finished biscuit can be broken cleanly along neat lines once cooled.

content.text: Petticoat tails are the wheel-shaped form of Scottish shortbread: one large round pressed flat, marked into segments, and baked whole. The name is thought to derive from the French petits gâteaux taillés, small cut cakes, or from the resemblance to the shaped panels of a full petticoat. Either way, the shape is the thing.
A small amount of milk makes the dough easier to bring together and gives slightly crisper edges. Use it sparingly: add just enough for the dough to hold.

items.prepNote: cold, plus a little more if needed

content.text: Combine the {{plain-flour}} and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl. Rub in the cold {{unsalted-butter}} with your fingertips until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Add the {{whole-milk}} a little at a time, pressing the dough together until it just holds. It should be smooth but not sticky. Wrap and chill for 20 minutes.

Preheat the oven to 150°C fan. On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough into a circle about 20 cm across and 8 mm thick. Slide onto a lined baking tray. Press a small glass or cutter into the centre to mark a circle about 5 cm across (do not cut all the way through).
from the outer edge to the inner circle to mark eight wedges. Crimp the edge with your fingers or press with a fork.

Bake for 22–25 minutes, until pale gold and just dry to the touch across the surface. The shortbread will feel soft in the centre when it comes out; it firms as it cools. Leave it on the tray for 10 minutes before breaking along the scored lines.
